request.question: Who wrote the play "...but the clouds..." starring Billie Whitelaw and Ronald Pickup?

title: Leo the Last
Make Mine Mink
Charlie Bubbles
Rockaby
Rough for Radio II
... but the clouds ...
Hotel Paradiso (film)
Footfalls
Ghost Trio (play)
Ronald Pickup
sentences: Leo the Last is a 1970 British drama film directed by John Boorman, based on the play "The Prince" by George Tabori, starring Marcello Mastroianni and Billie Whitelaw.
Make Mine Mink is a 1960 British comedy farce film directed by Robert Asher and featuring Terry-Thomas, Athene Seyler, Hattie Jacques and Billie Whitelaw.
The screenplay concerns a group of eccentric misfits who go on a spree, stealing mink coats for charity in a Robin Hood-style gang.
It was based on the play "Breath of Spring" by Peter Coke, and its sequels.
Seyer and Elspeth Duxbury reprised their stage roles from the London production of "Breath of Spring".
Charlie Bubbles is a 1967 British comedy-drama film starring Billie Whitelaw and Albert Finney, and also featuring a young Liza Minnelli.
It was listed to compete at the 1968 Cannes Film Festival, but the festival was cancelled due to the events of May 1968 in France.
Rockaby is a short one-woman play by Samuel Beckett.

It was written in English in 1980, at the request of Daniel Labeille, who produced it on behalf of "Programs in the Arts", State University of New York, for a festival and symposium in commemoration of Beckett's 75th birthday.
The play premiered on April 8, 1981 at the State University of New York at Buffalo, starring Billie Whitelaw and directed by Alan Schneider.
A documentary film, "Rockaby", by D. A. Pennebaker and Chris Hegedus records the rehearsal process and the first performance.
This production went on to be performed at the Annex at La MaMa Experimental Theatre Club, and, in December 1982, at the Cottesloe, Royal National Theatre, London.
Rough for Radio II is a radio play by Samuel Beckett.
It was written in French in 1961 as Pochade radiophonique and published in "Minuit" 16, November 1975.
Beckett translated the work into English shortly before its broadcast on BBC Radio 3 on 13 April 1976.
Martin Esslin directed Harold Pinter (Animator), Billie Whitelaw (Stenographer) and Patrick Magee (Fox).
The English-language version was first published in "Ends and Odds" (Grove, 1976, Faber, 1977) as Radio II.
... but the clouds ... is a television play by Samuel Beckett.
Beckett wrote it between October–November 1976 “to replace a film of "Play" which the BBC had sent [him] for approval (and which he had rejected)” due to “the poor quality of the film”.
Donald McWhinnie directed Billie Whitelaw and Ronald Pickup.
It was first broadcast on 17 April 1977 as part of a programme of three Beckett plays entitled ‘Shades’ on BBC2.
It was first published in "Ends and Odds" (Faber) 1977.
An early title for the piece was Poetry only love.
Hotel Paradiso is a Metrocolor 1966 film released by Metro-Goldwyn-Mayer in Panavision.
It was directed by Peter Glenville and based on the play "L'Hôtel du libre échange" by Maurice Desvallières and Georges Feydeau.
The film allowed Alec Guinness to reprise the role he had played in the London West End theatre production of Hotel Paradiso, which opened at the Winter Garden Theatre, Drury Lane, London.
on 2 May 1956.
Guinness played alongside Martita Hunt (Angelique), Irene Worth (Marcelle), Frank Pettingell (Cot), Kenneth Williams (Maxime) and Billie Whitelaw (Victoire).
Douglas Byng also reprised his part from the stage play.
Footfalls is a play by Samuel Beckett.
It was written in English, between 2 March and December 1975 and was first performed at the Royal Court Theatre as part of the Samuel Beckett Festival, on May 20, 1976 directed by Beckett himself.
Billie Whitelaw, for whom the piece had been written, played May whilst Rose Hill voiced the mother.
Ghost Trio is a television play, written in English by Samuel Beckett.
It was written in 1975, taped in October 1976 and the first broadcast was on BBC2 on 17 April 1977 as part of the "Lively Arts" programme Beckett himself entitled "Shades".
Donald McWhinnie directed (supervised by Beckett) with Ronald Pickup and Billie Whitelaw.
The play’s original title was to be Tryst.
"On Beckett’s notebook, the word was crossed out vigorously and the new title "Ghost Trio" written next to it.
On the title page of the BBC script the same handwritten title change can be found, indicating that it must have been corrected at the very last minute."
Ronald Alfred Pickup (born 7 June 1940) is an English actor who has been active in television and film since 1964.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Produce a compact, structured first-hop summary that helps the next agent fetch the missing second-hop facts. The summary must be one labeled block with these four short sections (each 1–4 lines):

1) Evidence found — For each concrete target term or name mentioned in the passages, list the target and which passage(s) mention it, and quote a short verbatim excerpt (≤15 words) or any exact numeric/date token from the passage. Preserve the quoted text and any numeric/date formatting exactly as it appears in the passages. Include passage identifier(s) exactly as given (title or index).

2) Ambiguities — Enumerate any names or terms that correspond to multiple candidate pages or where the passages return multiple variants or conflicting values. For each ambiguous item, list the candidate labels or note the conflict using only tokens from the passages where possible.

3) Bridge / missing fact — State exactly which relation(s) or fact(s) needed to answer the question are not present or are unresolved in the provided passages (e.g., birth date, publication year, author of adaptation, founding year, award count). Be explicit and concise.

4) Retrieval direction — Provide 2–4 concrete next-hop retrievals: specific page titles or disambiguation pages, or precise search queries the next agent should run to fetch the missing bridge facts. Use actionable, precise phrasing (page title or quoted query). End this section with an explicit deferral of the final answer when the passages do not unambiguously supply all required facts.

Formatting and behavior rules:
- Produce only the single field `summary` as the output. The `summary` text must follow the four labeled sections above.
- Always quote verbatim evidence (names, short excerpts, dates, numbers) exactly as in the passages; do not reformat numeric/date tokens.
- Do NOT produce a final answer here unless every required fact to resolve the question is present and unambiguous in the provided passages (i.e., each named entity corresponds to a single candidate and the needed relation value is explicitly present). If facts are unambiguous and complete, you may state that and include a concise final conclusion in the `summary`; otherwise explicitly defer (for example: "Final answer deferred pending retrieval of the targets below.").
- If the natural summary would otherwise be a single bare token or name, append a one-line bridge clause that identifies the missing relation and at least one retrieval target rather than ending with the bare token.
- Keep phrasing concise and factual; favor short lines, source-linked evidence excerpts, and explicit next-step instructions. Do not attempt to fetch pages yourself—only list retrieval targets/queries.

Example structure to follow in the `summary` field (use passage text and titles exactly):
Evidence found: ...
Ambiguities: ...
Bridge / missing fact: ...
Retrieval direction: ...

End of instructions.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Required output format and constraints:
- Produce exactly one field named `query`. Its value must be a single short, keyword-style search string (no full sentences, no explanations, no assertions, no user-facing clarifications).
- The query must be terse (preferably <= 12 words) and contain no clause punctuation or sentence grammar (no question marks, no leading "who/what is", no verbs forming assertions).
- Use only tokens that are visible in `question` and/or `summary_1`, except you may insert at most one minimal neutral connector token to target bridging evidence (allowed connector tokens include, but are not limited to: artist, album, director, who directed, biography, introduction, genre, regular season). Do not invent new entity names or facts.
- When the inputs present multiple entities or candidate items that must be linked (for example two titles, two names, or an anchor title plus a candidate), include both entity tokens from the inputs and a single neutral connector token between or adjacent to them to indicate the evidence bridge to retrieve (e.g., "EntityA EntityB director").
- If the question or summary includes structured tokens relevant to the bridge (years, league IDs, edition names), include those tokens plus a concise cue (for example year + league + "regular season") as part of the short query.
- You may perform light normalization of obvious misspellings in entity names or technical terms drawn from the visible inputs (fix clear typos), but do not invent alternative candidate entities or assert corrected identities beyond the token-level fix.
- If it is not possible to form a fully disambiguating query from visible tokens, still emit the best compact retrieval query possible using only visible tokens and an allowed connector; do not ask the user for clarification at this step. (Downstream retrieval will be attempted first; user clarification is allowed only after retrieval fails.)

Produce only the `query` field value (the single short search string) and nothing else.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Instructions for producing `summary` (single text field only):
- Output exactly one concise summary string (no extra fields, labels, or metadata). Keep the summary to 1–2 short sentences unless the evidence forces a single-token identification (see below).
- Primary goal: integrate the first-hop identity in `context` with facts found in `passages` so the final answer agent has the explicit, supported linkage it needs.

When the question requires linking the context entity to information in the passages and the passages supply the bridging fact:
- Produce a concise (1–2 sentence) integrated summary that (1) names the context entity (using the form present in `context`), (2) names the supporting entity/attribute found in `passages` (using the form present in the inputs), and (3) states the explicit relation or fact (membership, creator attribution, partnership, founding/establishment, award, etc.) exactly as supported by the inputs. Use only facts present in `context` and `passages` and avoid introducing any unsupported details.

Name and attribution formatting:
- If a canonical multi-token name (given + family name or full official name) appears in the inputs, use that full form in the summary; do not truncate to a surname or partial name.
- For questions about creator/author/credit, prefer the person-name attribution as given in the inputs over returning the title of a work or event.

Identification requests:
- If the question explicitly asks for a single identification (for example, asks "which X" or otherwise requests a named token or label), return exactly the canonical name(s) as presented in the passages. If multiple valid names are requested, return them comma-separated. Do not add explanatory clauses, relational fragments, or extra text in these cases—only the canonical name(s).

When evidence is missing or ambiguous:
- If the passages do not supply a clear bridging fact, state that succinctly (e.g., "No supporting evidence in the provided passages" or "Unclear from the provided sources"). Retain uncertainty rather than asserting unsupported facts.

Conciseness and fidelity constraints:
- Keep the summary short and factual (1–2 sentences). Do not expand into long biographies, exhaustive lists, or unrelated details.
- Do not assert facts that are not present in `context` and `passages`.
- Avoid long verbatim copying of passage text, but short verbatim names or phrases necessary for fidelity are allowed.
- Treat the `context` field as the primary first-hop identity to be linked to `passages` when integrating evidence.

Produce the `summary` now based only on the supplied `question`, `context`, and `passages`.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Use summary_1 and summary_2 as the sole evidential basis for the final answer; give greatest weight to summary_2 (the later, post-retrieval integrated evidence). Do not introduce facts from outside these summaries.

Output rules (apply in order):

1) If summary_1 and summary_2 clearly and unambiguously agree on the same concise answer, set the value of `answer` to exactly one minimal string and nothing else:
   - For a binary yes/no conclusion, emit the single lowercase token: yes or no.
   - For any other concise label, emit that label exactly as it appears in the summaries (trim leading/trailing whitespace and strip any surrounding punctuation or wrapper syntax such as surrounding parentheses, quotes, or JSON-like braces). Do not add words, sentences, punctuation, quotes, brackets, or any other surrounding text.

2) Preserve multi-token labels: if any visible summary contains a multi-token form of the answer (for example a full personal name, a multi-word company or place), output that full multi-token form verbatim. Only shorten a label if no multi-token form appears in either summary.

3) Company/developer preference: when the question requests a company or developer and the summaries show a subordinate studio together with an explicit parent/publisher label (e.g., in parentheses or with phrasing like "published by"), choose the parent/publisher name as the single minimal answer string. If no parent/publisher is explicitly indicated, choose the best-supported developer/studio name from the summaries.

4) If the summaries conflict or are ambiguous, do NOT apply the unanimous-agreement rule in (1). Instead, use summary_2 as the primary basis and emit the minimal concise string supported by summary_2, applying rules (2) and (3) as relevant. If summary_2 provides no concise supported answer, set `answer` to the minimal explicit token "unknown".

Formatting constraints (must always be followed):
- Produce exactly one field: `answer`.
- The value of `answer` must be a single minimal string (no surrounding text, punctuation, explanation, or structural wrappers).
- Do not output extra fields, labels, or markup.

Additional guidance:
- If summary_1 describes an earlier unresolved bridge but summary_2 provides a resolved, supported conclusion, use the conclusion from summary_2.
- If a concise answer string can be drawn verbatim from the summaries, prefer that verbatim text over paraphrase.

End.

response.hop1_query: Who wrote the play "...but the clouds..." starring Billie Whitelaw and Ronald Pickup?
hop1_docs: ... but the clouds ... | ... but the clouds ... is a television play by Samuel Beckett.  Beckett wrote it between October–November 1976 “to replace a film of "Play" which the BBC had sent [him] for approval (and which he had rejected)” due to “the poor quality of the film”.  Donald McWhinnie directed Billie Whitelaw and Ronald Pickup.  It was first broadcast on 17 April 1977 as part of a programme of three Beckett plays entitled ‘Shades’ on BBC2.  It was first published in "Ends and Odds" (Faber) 1977.  An early title for the piece was Poetry only love.

Ghost Trio (play) | Ghost Trio is a television play, written in English by Samuel Beckett.  It was written in 1975, taped in October 1976 and the first broadcast was on BBC2 on 17 April 1977 as part of the "Lively Arts" programme Beckett himself entitled "Shades".  Donald McWhinnie directed (supervised by Beckett) with Ronald Pickup and Billie Whitelaw.  The play’s original title was to be Tryst.  "On Beckett’s notebook, the word was crossed out vigorously and the new title "Ghost Trio" written next to it.  On the title page of the BBC script the same handwritten title change can be found, indicating that it must have been corrected at the very last minute."
Leo the Last | Leo the Last is a 1970 British drama film directed by John Boorman, based on the play "The Prince" by George Tabori, starring Marcello Mastroianni and Billie Whitelaw.

Private Schulz | Private Schulz is a BBC television comedy drama serial set mostly in Germany, during and immediately after World War II.  It stars Michael Elphick in the title role and Ian Richardson playing various parts.  Other notable actors included Tony Caunter, Billie Whitelaw, Billy Murray and Mark Wingett.
Ronald Pickup | Ronald Alfred Pickup (born 7 June 1940) is an English actor who has been active in television and film since 1964.

Shadey | Shadey is a 1985 British comedy film directed by Philip Saville and starring Antony Sher, Billie Whitelaw and Patrick Macnee.  The screenplay concerns a man with clairvoyant qualities who is recruited by British intelligence for a secret mission.  The film grossed over $65,817.
Tangiers (1982 film) | Tangier is a 1982 American-Moroccan thriller film directed by Michael E. Briant and starring Ronny Cox, Billie Whitelaw and Glynis Barber.
response.summary_1:
Evidence found:
- "... but the clouds ..." — [1]: "... but the clouds ... is a television play by Samuel Beckett."
- "Samuel Beckett" — [1]: "television play by Samuel Beckett."
- "Donald McWhinnie" — [1]: "Donald McWhinnie directed Billie Whitelaw and Ronald Pickup."
- "Billie Whitelaw" — [1]: "Donald McWhinnie directed Billie Whitelaw and Ronald Pickup."
- "Ronald Pickup" — [1]: "Donald McWhinnie directed Billie Whitelaw and Ronald Pickup."
- "Ronald Pickup and Billie Whitelaw" — [2]: "Ronald Pickup and Billie Whitelaw."
- "17 April 1977" (broadcast date) — [1]: "17 April 1977"

Ambiguities:
- "Ghost Trio" vs "... but the clouds ...": both passages ([1], [2]) reference Beckett, Pickup and Whitelaw in the same BBC program, which could cause title conflation.
- Title formatting: the play title uses ellipses ("... but the clouds ...") which may have variant listings.

Bridge / missing fact:
- None for the question asked: the author of "... but the clouds ..." is explicitly stated in the provided passages.

Retrieval direction:
- No further retrieval required; final answer: Samuel Beckett wrote "... but the clouds ...".
response.hop2_query: ... but the clouds ... Samuel Beckett author
hop2_docs: LÉ Samuel Beckett (P61) | LÉ "Samuel Beckett" (P61) is a "Samuel Beckett"-class offshore patrol vessel (OPV) of the Irish Naval Service.  The ship was launched in November 2013 and commissioned in May 2014.  She is named after Irish playwright and author Samuel Beckett.

... but the clouds ... | ... but the clouds ... is a television play by Samuel Beckett.  Beckett wrote it between October–November 1976 “to replace a film of "Play" which the BBC had sent [him] for approval (and which he had rejected)” due to “the poor quality of the film”.  Donald McWhinnie directed Billie Whitelaw and Ronald Pickup.  It was first broadcast on 17 April 1977 as part of a programme of three Beckett plays entitled ‘Shades’ on BBC2.  It was first published in "Ends and Odds" (Faber) 1977.  An early title for the piece was Poetry only love.

The Lost Ones (Beckett) | The Lost Ones is the English translation of "Le Dépeupleur", a short story abandoned by Samuel Beckett in 1966 and completed in 1970.  "The Lost Ones" was published in French in 1970 and translated by the author in 1971.

Samuel Beckett Bridge | Samuel Beckett Bridge (Irish: "Droichead Samuel Beckett" ) is a cable-stayed bridge in Dublin that joins Sir John Rogerson's Quay on the south side of the River Liffey to Guild Street and North Wall Quay in the Docklands area.

The Unnamable (novel) | The Unnamable is a 1953 novel by Samuel Beckett.  It is the third and final entry in Beckett's "Trilogy" of novels, which begins with Molloy followed by Malone Dies.  It was originally published in French as "L'Innommable" and later adapted by the author into English.  Grove Press published the English edition in 1958.

Murphy (novel) | Murphy, first published in 1938, is an avant-garde novel as well as the third work of prose fiction by the Irish author and dramatist Samuel Beckett.  The book was Beckett's second published prose work after the short-story collection "More Pricks than Kicks" (published in 1934) and his unpublished first novel "Dream of Fair to Middling Women" (published posthumously in 1992).  It was written in English, rather than the French of much of Beckett's later writing.  After many rejections, it was published by Routledge on the recommendation of Beckett's painter friend Jack Butler Yeats.

Samuel Beckett Award | The Samuel Beckett Award is a British award set up in 1983 and awarded to writers and directors, who in the opinion of a committee of critics, producers and publishers, showed excellence in writing or directing for the performing arts.  The award was established in honour of Irish avant-garde novelist, playwright, theatre director, and poet Samuel Beckett and in recognition of his distinctive contribution to world theatre and literature.
response.summary_2: "... but the clouds ..." is a television play written by Samuel Beckett.
